questions.question: How does a ViT turn a 224×224 image into a sequence of tokens ready for the transformer encoder?
options: It flattens the entire 3×224×224 tensor into one long vector and projects it to the model dimension

It applies a Conv2d with kernel_size=stride=patch_size, projecting the image into a grid of non-overlapping patch embeddings in a single operation — a 224×224 image with patch_size=16 produces a 14×14=196 token sequence, each of dimension 768
It runs a CNN backbone first and uses the final feature map as the token sequence
It sorts pixel intensities into a fixed-length codebook before feeding them to the transformer
questions.explanation: The first layer of ViT is a Conv2d with kernel_size=stride=16, turning a 224×224 image into a 14×14 grid of 16×16 patches, each projected to dim=768. That single conv does both patchification and linear projection. The result is 196 tokens that a standard transformer encoder processes. Every ViT variant (DeiT, Swin, MAE) changes what happens after this step.

questions.question: A `PatchEmbedding` layer is configured with `patch_size=16` and `image_size=224`. How many patch tokens does it produce, and what is the formula?
options: 16 tokens (the patch size itself)
224 tokens (one per pixel row)
256 tokens (16² — the patches along each axis squared)
196 tokens, computed as (image_size ÷ patch_size)² = (224 ÷ 16)² = 14² = 196 non-overlapping patches tiled across the image
questions.explanation: The stride-equals-kernel-size convolution in PatchEmbedding tiles the image with non-overlapping patches. Along each spatial axis the number of patches is image_size ÷ patch_size = 224 ÷ 16 = 14. Squaring gives 196 tokens total. Halving the patch size to 8 quadruples the token count to 784 and quadruples self-attention cost — the patch-size trade-off is the most impactful hyperparameter in ViT.

questions.question: In the `Block` forward method, attention output `a` is added to the un-normalised `x` before LayerNorm is applied in the MLP branch: `x = x + a; x = x + self.mlp(self.ln2(x))`. What design pattern does this implement, and why does it matter for training deep ViTs?
options: Post-LayerNorm with residual connections — the original 2017 transformer design that is stable up to about 12 layers
Weight tying between the attention and MLP sublayers to reduce the parameter count of each block

Pre-LayerNorm with residual connections: LayerNorm is applied to the input before each sublayer, and the sublayer output is added back to the un-normalised residual stream — this keeps gradient norms stable in deep networks without needing a warmup schedule
Gradient checkpointing: the intermediate `a` tensor is recomputed during the backward pass to reduce peak activation memory
questions.explanation: Pre-LayerNorm (`x + sublayer(LN(x))`) differs from post-LN (`LN(x + sublayer(x))`) in where normalisation sits relative to the residual. Pre-LN keeps the residual stream un-normalised so gradients flow directly without passing through LN, enabling stable training of 12-24+ layer models without warmup. Every ViT and every modern LLM uses pre-LN for this reason.

questions.question: In the `ViT` class, the positional embedding is initialised with shape `(1, num_patches + 1, dim)` rather than `(1, num_patches, dim)`. Why the +1?
options: Because the CLS token is prepended to the patch sequence and also needs a positional embedding; index 0 of the positional embedding corresponds to the CLS position, and indices 1…num_patches correspond to the spatial patches
To accommodate variable batch sizes via broadcasting along the batch axis
Because the last patch in the flattened sequence is split into two sub-patches for boundary handling
To leave a reserved slot for the distillation token used in DeiT training
questions.explanation: ViT prepends a learned [CLS] token to the patch sequence, making the full sequence length num_patches + 1 = 197 for a standard ViT-B/16 on 224×224 input. The positional embedding must cover all 197 positions. In the ViT forward method, `self.pos_embed` is broadcast over the batch and added to the concatenated [CLS + patches] tensor. The CLS position at index 0 learns to encode where the global token sits relative to the patches.

questions.question: Why did the original ViT paper need JFT-300M pretraining to beat ResNet on ImageNet-1k, and why does DeiT not?
options: The original ViT paper contained implementation errors that DeiT corrected, independently of dataset size

ViT has weaker inductive biases than CNNs — no locality or translation equivariance baked in — so it needs more data to learn spatial structure. DeiT showed that with strong augmentation (RandAugment, Mixup, CutMix), stochastic depth, and optional CNN distillation, ViTs train to 81%+ on ImageNet-1k alone
DeiT uses a different attention mechanism that is more sample-efficient than scaled dot-product attention
The original ViT paper intentionally held back training tricks to leave room for follow-up work
questions.explanation: ViT's lack of inductive bias means the network must learn locality and translation invariance from data alone. With 1M ImageNet images that is hard; with 300M JFT images it is easy. DeiT replaced the data with training-recipe improvements: heavy augmentation, stochastic depth, and a CNN teacher for distillation — all of which act as inductive-bias surrogates. Every modern ViT training recipe descends from DeiT.

questions.question: Pre-LayerNorm (`x = x + sublayer(LN(x))`) is used in modern transformers instead of the original post-LayerNorm (`x = LN(x + sublayer(x))`). Which is correct and why?
options: Pre-LN is used because it trains deeper networks stably without learning-rate warmup: the residual stream accumulates without passing through LN, so each sublayer operates on a normalised input while gradients flow unobstructed through the skip connection
Post-LN is the modern choice because it was validated on larger language models first and then adopted for ViT
The two formulations are mathematically identical for networks with residual connections and LayerNorm
Neither; modern transformers remove LayerNorm entirely and use RMSNorm in a post-sublayer position
questions.explanation: Post-LN was the original 2017 transformer and required careful warmup and small learning rates to train past 6–8 layers. Pre-LN (used by ViT, GPT-2+, every modern LLM) is numerically more stable: the residual stream accumulates without normalisation, keeping gradient norms well-behaved through depth. Every transformer deeper than 12 layers uses pre-LN.
